subtopics.title: Introduction to Data Structures and Algorithms

blocks.title: What are Data Structures?
blocks.content: A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data in a computer so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. Different data structures are suited for different tasks.

blocks.title: What are Algorithms?
blocks.content: An algorithm is a step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. It takes inputs, processes them, and produces outputs. Algorithms are implemented using data structures.

blocks.title: Data Structure Categories
rows: Elements are arranged in a sequence.
Elements are arranged in a hierarchical or network structure.

blocks.title: Steps to Choose a Data Structure
steps: Step 2: Identify the operations needed (insert, delete, search, sort).
Step 3: Consider the frequency and complexity of operations.
Step 4: Choose the data structure that best fits the requirements.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Data structures organize data for efficient access.
Algorithms are procedures for solving problems.
The right data structure can significantly improve performance.
Data structures can be linear (arrays, lists) or non-linear (trees, graphs).

subtopics.title: Arrays – The Most Basic Data Structure

blocks.title: What is an Array?
blocks.content: An array is a collection of elements of the same data type, stored in contiguous memory locations. Elements are accessed using an index (0-based in most languages).

blocks.title: Array Operations and Complexity
rows: Add an element at the end.

blocks.title: Trap: Array index out of bounds.
blocks.tip: Always ensure index is within range.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Arrays store elements contiguously.
Access is O(1) (constant time).
Insertion/deletion at the beginning is O(n).
Binary search works only on sorted arrays.

blocks.title: What is a Linked List?
blocks.content: A linked list is a linear data structure where each element (node) contains data and a pointer (reference) to the next node. Nodes are not stored contiguously in memory.

blocks.title: Types of Linked Lists
rows: Each node points to the next node.
Each node points to both next and previous nodes.
Last node points back to the first node.

blocks.title: Linked List Operations and Complexity
rows: Find element at a specific position.
Add node at the tail (with tail pointer).

blocks.title: Example: Singly Linked List
steps: class Node { int data; Node next; }

blocks.title: Trap: Forgetting to update the tail pointer.
blocks.wrong: Inserting at the end without updating tail.
blocks.correct: Always update tail when adding to the end.
blocks.tip: Maintain a tail pointer for O(1) insertion at the end.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Linked lists use dynamic memory allocation.
Insertion/deletion at the head is O(1).
Access/search is O(n) (sequential).
Doubly linked lists support traversal in both directions.
Circular linked lists are useful for round-robin scheduling.

blocks.title: What is a Stack?
blocks.content: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle. Elements are added (pushed) and removed (popped) from the top only.

blocks.title: Stack Operations
rows: Adds an element to the top of the stack.
Removes and returns the top element.
Returns the top element without removing it.
Checks if the stack is empty.

blocks.title: Example: Stack Implementation
given: A stack with operations push(), pop(), and peek().
blocks.answer: Stack follows LIFO: 30 is popped first.

blocks.title: Applications of Stacks
blocks.content:
1. Function call stack (recursion).
2. Expression evaluation (infix, postfix, prefix).
3. Undo/Redo operations.
4. Backtracking (e.g., maze solving).
5. Browser history.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing Stack and Queue.
blocks.correct: Stack follows LIFO; Queue follows FIFO.
blocks.tip: Stack = Last In, First Out; Queue = First In, First Out.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Stack = LIFO (Last In, First Out).
Operations: push() [O(1)], pop() [O(1)], peek() [O(1)].
Implementable using arrays or linked lists.
Widely used in recursion and expression evaluation.

blocks.title: What is a Queue?
blocks.content: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the FIFO (First In, First Out) principle. Elements are added at the rear (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue).

blocks.title: Queue Operations
rows: Adds an element to the rear of the queue.
Removes and returns the front element.
Returns the front element without removing it.
Checks if the queue is empty.

blocks.title: Example: Queue Implementation
given: A queue with operations enqueue() and dequeue().
blocks.answer: Queue follows FIFO: 10 is dequeued first.

blocks.title: Types of Queues
blocks.content:
1. **Simple Queue** – Basic FIFO.
2. **Circular Queue** – Reuses memory by connecting the rear to the front.
3. **Deque (Double-Ended Queue)** – Insert/delete from both ends.
4. **Priority Queue** – Elements are removed based on priority.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing Queue and Priority Queue.
blocks.correct: Priority Queue removes the element with the highest priority, not necessarily the oldest.
blocks.tip: Priority Queue uses a heap internally for ordering.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Queue = FIFO (First In, First Out).
Operations: enqueue() [O(1)], dequeue() [O(1)], front() [O(1)].
Circular queue saves memory.
Priority queue uses heap (highest priority first).

blocks.title: What is a Tree?
blocks.content: A tree is a non-linear data structure with a hierarchical structure. It consists of nodes connected by edges. The topmost node is the root, and nodes with no children are leaves.

blocks.title: Tree Terminology
rows: Any node on the path from root to the node.
Any node in the subtree of a node.
Number of edges from root to the node.
Number of edges from node to the deepest leaf.

blocks.title: Types of Trees
rows: Each node has at most two children.
Used in C++ STL, Java TreeMap.
Complete binary tree with heap property.
Tree for string storage and search.

blocks.title: Example: Binary Search Tree
blocks.answer: BST structure: root=50, left=30, right=70, etc.

blocks.title: Trap: BST vs Heap.
blocks.correct: Heap follows heap property (parent >= children in max-heap), not BST property.
blocks.tip: BST: Left < Parent < Right; Heap: Parent >= Children (max-heap).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Tree = Hierarchical structure with root and leaves.
BST: Left < Parent < Right for efficient search (O(log n)).
Self-balancing trees (AVL, Red-Black) ensure O(log n) performance.
Heaps are used for priority queues.

blocks.title: What is a Graph?
blocks.content: A graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. Graphs can represent networks like social media, road maps, or communication networks.

blocks.title: Graph Terminology
rows: A single point in the graph.
Two vertices connected by an edge.
Number of edges incident to a vertex.
Sequence of vertices connected by edges.
Path that starts and ends at the same vertex.

blocks.title: Graph Representations
rows: Array of lists: each vertex has a list of neighbors.

blocks.title: Graph Traversal Algorithms
rows: Explores as far as possible along each branch.

blocks.title: Example: BFS Traversal
given: Graph: A-B, A-C, B-D, B-E, C-F
steps: Visit A, enqueue B, C → Queue: B, C
Visit B, enqueue D, E → Queue: C, D, E
Visit C, enqueue F → Queue: D, E, F
Visit D, E, F in order.
blocks.answer: BFS: A, B, C, D, E, F

blocks.title: Trap: BFS and DFS are the same.
blocks.wrong: BFS and DFS use the same data structure.
blocks.correct: BFS uses a Queue; DFS uses a Stack.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Graph = Vertices + Edges.
BFS = Queue, Level-by-level.
DFS = Stack/Recursion, Branch-first.
Adjacency List is more memory-efficient for sparse graphs.
Adjacency Matrix is faster for dense graphs.

subtopics.title: Sorting Algorithms – Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Merge, Quick

blocks.title: Trap: Quick Sort worst-case.
blocks.wrong: Quick Sort always runs in O(n log n).
blocks.correct: Quick Sort can degrade to O(n²) if the pivot is poorly chosen.
blocks.tip: Use randomized pivot or median-of-three for better performance.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Bubble, Selection, Insertion: O(n²) (simple, slow).
Merge Sort: O(n log n), stable, not in-place.
Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, unstable, in-place.
Heap Sort: O(n log n), unstable, in-place.
Merge Sort is preferred for large data sets (stable).
Quick Sort is preferred for average-case speed.

subtopics.title: Searching Algorithms – Linear and Binary Search

blocks.title: Searching Algorithms
rows: Divides the search space in half.

blocks.title: Example: Binary Search
blocks.find: Find the index using Binary Search.

blocks.title: Trap: Binary Search on unsorted array.
blocks.wrong: Using Binary Search on an unsorted array.
blocks.correct: Binary Search only works on sorted arrays.
blocks.tip: Always sort the array before applying Binary Search.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Linear Search: O(n), works on any array.
Binary Search: O(log n), requires sorted array.
Binary Search is much faster for large sorted datasets.
Implement Binary Search iteratively or recursively.

blocks.title: What is Complexity Analysis?
blocks.content: Complexity analysis measures the time and space required by an algorithm as a function of the input size (n). It helps in comparing algorithms and choosing the most efficient one.

blocks.title: Common Big O Notations
rows: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Nested loops

blocks.title: Example: Analysing Complexity
given: A nested loop: for i in 1..n: for j in 1..n: print(i,j)
steps: Inner loop runs n times for each outer iteration.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing average and worst-case complexity.
blocks.wrong: Quick Sort is always O(n log n).
blocks.correct: Quick Sort is O(n log n) on average, but O(n²) in the worst case.
blocks.tip: Always specify best, average, and worst-case complexity.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Big O describes the upper bound (worst-case) of growth rate.
O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2^n) < O(n!).
Space complexity measures memory usage.
Choose algorithms with lower time and space complexity for large inputs.

blocks.title: What is a Hash Table?
blocks.content: A hash table (or hash map) is a data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function. It provides O(1) average time complexity for insertion, deletion, and lookup.

blocks.title: Hash Table Components
rows: Converts a key into an index in the array.
Two keys produce the same index.

blocks.title: Collision Resolution Techniques
rows: Each index contains a linked list of colliding entries.
Uses a quadratic function to find the next slot.
Uses a second hash function for the step size.

blocks.title: Example: Hash Table Operations
steps: Lookup 'C' → hash to index 2, search the chain.
blocks.answer: Hash table with chaining handles collisions.

blocks.title: Trap: Hash Table worst-case performance.
blocks.correct: Hash Tables are O(1) on average, but can degrade to O(n) with poor hash function or many collisions.
blocks.tip: Use a good hash function and load factor control.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Hash Table = Key-Value storage with O(1) average access.
Hash function maps keys to array indices.
Collisions are resolved using chaining or open addressing.
Load factor determines when to resize the table.

subtopics.title: Shortcuts and Memory Tricks for DSA

blocks.title: Shortcut 1 – Big O Order
blocks.content: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²).

blocks.title: Shortcut 2 – Linear vs Non-Linear
blocks.content:
Linear: Arrays, Lists, Stacks, Queues.
Non-Linear: Trees, Graphs.

blocks.title: Shortcut 3 – Sorting Memory
blocks.content:
Merge Sort = Stable, O(n log n), extra memory.
Quick Sort = Unstable, O(n log n) average, in-place.

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Arrays, Linked Lists, Stacks, Trees, Graphs – the five core data structures.

blocks.title: Shortcut Summary
points: Arrays: O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion at start.
Linked Lists: O(1) insertion/deletion at head, O(n) access.
Stacks: LIFO, O(1) push/pop.
Queues: FIFO, O(1) enqueue/dequeue.
Trees: Hierarchical, BST O(log n) search.
Graphs: BFS (Queue), DFS (Stack).
Sorting: O(n log n) for efficient sorts (Merge, Quick).
Hash Tables: O(1) average access.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Focus on the properties of data structures (LIFO/FIFO), complexity analysis (Big O), sorting algorithms, and traversal algorithms (BFS/DFS).

blocks.question: Which data structure follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle?
blocks.explanation: Stack follows LIFO. Queue follows FIFO.

blocks.question: Which data structure follows FIFO (First In, First Out) principle?
blocks.explanation: Queue follows FIFO. Stack follows LIFO.

blocks.question: What is the time complexity of Binary Search on a sorted array of size n?
blocks.explanation: Binary Search divides the search space in half each step, giving O(log n) complexity.

blocks.question: Which sorting algorithm has the worst-case time complexity of O(n²)?
blocks.explanation: Bubble Sort has O(n²) worst-case. Quick Sort also has O(n²) worst-case, but Bubble Sort is the classic example.

blocks.question: Which data structure is used in BFS (Breadth-First Search)?
blocks.explanation: BFS uses a Queue to explore vertices level by level.

blocks.question: Which data structure is used in DFS (Depth-First Search)?
blocks.explanation: DFS uses a Stack (or recursion) to explore deep branches first.

blocks.question: What is the time complexity of Merge Sort?
blocks.explanation: Merge Sort has O(n log n) time complexity in all cases.

blocks.question: Which data structure is a non-linear data structure?
blocks.explanation: Tree is a non-linear data structure. Arrays, Linked Lists, and Stacks are linear.

blocks.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?
blocks.explanation: Array access by index is O(1) (constant time).

blocks.question: Which sorting algorithm is stable?
blocks.explanation: Merge Sort is stable. Quick Sort and Heap Sort are unstable.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review the differences between stacks and queues, complexity notations (Big O), and sorting algorithms. Understand BFS vs DFS and the properties of trees and graphs.

blocks.title: Final Revision – DSA
points: Stack: LIFO, push/pop O(1).
Queue: FIFO, enqueue/dequeue O(1).
Array: O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion at start.
Linked List: O(1) insertion at head, O(n) access.
Tree: Hierarchical, BST O(log n) search.
Graph: BFS (Queue), DFS (Stack).
Big O: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²).
Sorting: Bubble/Selection/Insertion O(n²), Merge/Quick/Heap O(n log n).
Search: Linear O(n), Binary O(log n).
Hash Table: O(1) average access.
